questions.question_html: Which of the following fractions is the smallest?

options.explanation: {tex}\frac{11}{10}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: Decimal 8.125 is equal to the fraction ________.
questions.solution_html: {tex}\frac{65}{8}{/tex} or {tex}8 \frac{1}{8}{/tex} (because 8.125 = {tex}\frac{8125}{1000}{/tex})

questions.question_html: The fraction {tex}14 \frac{2}{5}{/tex} is equal to 14.2

options.explanation: False

questions.question_html:
Fill in the blanks using > or < :
{tex}\frac{8}{45}{/tex} ________ {tex}\frac{16}{89}{/tex}
questions.solution_html:
<
{tex}\frac{8}{45}=\frac{8 \times 2}{45 \times 2}=\frac{16}{90}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{16}{90}<\frac{16}{89}{/tex} so, {tex}\frac{8}{45}<\frac{16}{89} {/tex}
Another method: 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 89 = 712 and 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 = 720
As 712 < 720, therefore {tex}\frac{8}{45}<\frac{16}{89}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Add the fractions {tex}5 \frac{3}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{16}{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
{tex}5 \frac{3}{8}+\frac{5}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{43}{8}+\frac{5}{16}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{43 \times 2}{8 \times 2}+\frac{5}{16}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{86}{16}+\frac{5}{16}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{86+5}{16}=\frac{91}{16}{/tex} = {tex}5 \frac{11}{16}{/tex}

questions.question_html:
What is wrong in the following?
{tex}\frac{7}{4}+\frac{5}{2}=\frac{7\ +\ 5}{4\ +\ 2}=\frac{12}{6}=2{/tex}
questions.solution_html:
Writing {tex}\frac{7}{4}+\frac{5}{2}=\frac{7\ +\ 5}{4\ +\ 2} {/tex} is wrong. It should be as follows:
{tex}\frac{7}{4}+\frac{5}{2}=\frac{7}{4}+\frac{10}{4}{/tex}(Converting into like fractions)
{tex}\frac{7\ +\ 10}{4}=\frac{17}{4}{/tex} (Only numerators are added)

questions.question_html: The fraction which is not equal to {tex}\frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex} is

options.explanation:
since {tex}\frac{9}{15}=\frac{9 \div 3}{15 \div 3}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, which is not equal to {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{9}{15}{/tex} is the required fraction.

questions.question_html: The two consecutive integers between which the fraction {tex}\frac { 5 } { 7 }{/tex} lies are

options.explanation:
Since we know that a proper fraction whose numerator is less than its denominator always lies between 0 and 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex}, which is also a proper fraction, must lie between 0 and 1.

questions.question_html: When {tex}1 \over4{/tex} is written with denominator as 12, its numerator is

options.explanation:
since {tex}\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{3}{/tex}
[By multiplying the numerator and denominator by 3 to get 12 as the denominator] = {tex}\frac{3}{12}{/tex}, which shows that the required numerator is 3.

questions.question_html: Which of the following is not in the lowest form?

options.explanation:
In the case of {tex}\frac{7}{5}{/tex}, the common factor of 7 and 5 is 1
i.e., already given in the simplest form.
Similarly,
ln {tex}\frac{15}{20} {/tex}, the common factor of 15 and 20 is 4
ln {tex}\frac{13}{33}{/tex} the common factor of 13 and 33 is
i.e., already given in the simplest form
ln {tex}\frac{27}{28}{/tex}, the common factor of 27 and 28 is
i.e., already given in the simplest form.
So, the above cases show that {tex}\frac{15}{20}{/tex} is not in the lowest form.

questions.question_html: If {tex}\frac{5}{8}=\frac{20}{p}{/tex}, then value of p is

options.explanation:
we have given, {tex}\frac{5}{8}=\frac{20}{p}{/tex}, To find the value of p, we must multiply the numerator and denominator by 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{8}=\frac{5\ \times \ 4}{8\ \times\ 4}=\frac{20}{32}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{20}{32}=\frac{20}{p}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The value of p is 32.

questions.question_html: Which of the following is not equal to the others?

options.explanation:
Firstly, we will simplify all the fractions into their lowest form, we get,
{tex}\frac{6}{8}=\frac{6 \div 2}{8 \div 2}= \frac{3}{4}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{12}{16}=\frac{12\ \div \ 4}{16\ \div \ 4}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{15}{25}=\frac{15 \div 5}{25 \div 5}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{18}{24}=\frac{18\ \div \ 6}{24\ \div \ 6}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
Thus, on comparing the above cases, we observe that {tex}\frac{3}{5}=\frac{15}{25}{/tex} is not equal to the above given fractions.

questions.question_html: . Which of the following fractions is the greatest?

options.explanation:
We have given, {tex}\frac{5}{7}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}
since we know that, among all the fractions with the same numerator, the one with smaller denominator will be greatest ¡.e., {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} is the required fraction.

questions.question_html: Which of the given fractions is the smallest?

options.explanation:
We have given, {tex}\frac{7}{8}, \frac{9}{8}, \frac{3}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}
since we know that, among all the fractions with the same denominator, the one with smaller numerator will be smallest i.e., {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} is the required fraction.

questions.question_html: Sum of {tex}\frac{4}{17}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{15}{17}{/tex} is

options.explanation:
we have {tex}\frac{4}{17}+\frac{15}{17}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4\ +\ 15}{17}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Sum of like fractions = {tex}Sum \ of \ their \ numerator \over common \ denominator{/tex}]
{tex}=\frac{19}{17}{/tex}

questions.question_html: On subtracting {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} from {tex}\frac{19}{9}{/tex}, the result is

options.explanation:
According to question, {tex}\frac{19}{9}-\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{19\ -\ 5}{9}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Difference of like fraction = {tex}Difference \ of \ their\ numerator \over Common \ denominator{/tex}]
{tex}=\frac{14}{9}{/tex}

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{11}{7} {/tex} can be expressed in the form

options.explanation:
Dividing 11 by 7, we get the quotient = 1 and the remainder = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \frac{11}{7}=1 \frac{4}{7}{/tex}

questions.question_html: The mixed fraction {tex}5 \frac { 4 } { 7 }{/tex} can be expressed as

options.explanation: We have, {tex}5 \frac{4}{7}=\frac{(5 \times 7)+4}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{35+4}{7}=\frac{39}{7}{/tex}

questions.question_html: The decimal 0.238 is equal to the fraction

options.explanation:
We have given 0.238, which can be written in the form of
{tex}\frac{238}{1000}=\frac{238\div2}{1000\div2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{119}{500}{/tex}

questions.question_html: A number representing a part of a ________ is called a fraction.
A fraction with denominator greater than the numerator is called a ________ fraction.
Fractions with the same denominator are called ________ fractions.
{tex}13 \frac{5}{18}{/tex} is a ________ fraction.
{tex}\frac{18}{5} {/tex} is a/an ________ fraction.
{tex}\frac{7}{19}{/tex} is a ________ fraction.
{tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} are ________ proper fractions.
{tex}\frac{6}{11}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{6}{13}{/tex} are ________ proper fractions.
Fill in the blank: The fraction {tex}6 \over15{/tex} in simplest form is ________.
questions.solution_html: We have, {tex}\frac{6}{15}=\frac{6 \div 3}{15 \div 3}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}

questions.question_html: The fraction {tex}17 \over34{/tex} in simplest form is ________.
questions.solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
We have,  {tex}\frac{17}{34}=\frac{17 \div 17}{34 \div 17}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{18}{135}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{90}{675}{/tex} are proper, unlike and ________ fractions.
Fill in the blank: {tex}8 \frac{2}{7}{/tex} is equal to the improper fraction ________.
questions.solution_html: {tex} 8 \frac{2}{7}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(8 \times 7)+2}{7}=\frac{56+2}{7}=\frac{58}{7}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: {tex}\frac{87}{7}{/tex} is equal to the mixed fraction ________.
questions.solution_html: {tex}12 \frac{3}{7}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: Decimal 16.25 is equal to the fraction ________.
questions.solution_html:
We have, 
{tex}16.25=\frac{1625}{100}=\frac{1625 \div 25}{100 \div 25}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{65}{4}{/tex} or {tex}16 \frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: {tex}\frac{17}{9}+\frac{41}{9}{/tex} = ________.
questions.solution_html: {tex}\frac{17}{9}+\frac{41}{9}=\frac{17+41}{9}=\frac{58}{9}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: {tex}\frac{67}{14}-\frac{24}{14}{/tex} = ________.
questions.solution_html: {tex}\frac{67}{14}-\frac{24}{14}=\frac{67-24}{14}=\frac{43}{14} {/tex}

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{17}{2}+3 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = ________.
{tex}9 \frac{1}{4}-\frac{5}{4}{/tex} = ________.
The value of 50 coins of 50 paisa = ₹ ________.
Fractions with the same numerator are called like fractions.

options.explanation:
False
Because, fractions with same denominators are called like fractions.

questions.question_html: Fraction {tex}18 \over39{/tex} is in its lowest form.

options.explanation:
False
since the common factor of 18 and 39 is 3 and its simplest form is {tex}\frac{18\ \div \ 3}{39\ \div\ 3}=\frac{6}{13}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Fractions {tex}\frac{15}{39} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{45}{117}{/tex} are equivalent fractions.

options.explanation: True

questions.question_html: The sum of two fractions is always a fraction.

options.explanation:
True
Let {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} are two fractions. When we add them, we get {tex}\frac{2}{3}+\frac{4}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2\ +\ 4}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{1}{/tex}, which is a fraction.

questions.question_html: The result obtained by subtracting a fraction from another fraction is necessarily a fraction.

options.explanation:
False
Let {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} are two fractions. When we subtract {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} from {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, we get {tex}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3\ -\ 4}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{6}{/tex}
Which is not a fraction.

questions.question_html: If a whole or an object is divided into a number of equal parts, then each part represents a fraction.

options.explanation: True

questions.question_html: The place value of a digit at the tenths place is 10 times the same digit at the ones place.

options.explanation:
False
since, the place value of a digit at the tenths place is {tex}\frac{1}{10} {/tex} times the same digit at the ones place.
i.e., {tex}\frac{x}{10}=10 x{/tex}, which is not possible

questions.question_html: The place value of a digit at the hundredths place is {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} times the same digit at the tenths place.

options.explanation: True

questions.question_html: The decimal 23.2 = {tex}23 \frac{2}{5}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False
we have, {tex}23.2=\frac{232}{10}=\frac{116}{5}{/tex}

{tex}=23 \frac{1}{5}{/tex}

questions.question_html: The fraction represented by the shaded portion in the adjoining figure is {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}

options.explanation:
True.
Total number of parts in a given figure is 8 and shaded parts are 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction is {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: The fraction represented by the unshaded portion in the adjoining figure is {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False
Total number of parts in a given figure is 9 from which unshaded parts is 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction is {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{25}{19}+\frac{6}{19}=\frac{31}{38}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False {tex}\frac{25}{19}+\frac{6}{19}=\frac{25\ +\ 6}{19}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{31}{19} \neq \frac{31}{38}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The above condition is false.

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{8}{18}-\frac{8}{15}=\frac{8}{3}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False

{tex}\because{/tex} The L.C.M. of 18 and 15 is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 5 = 90
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{8}{18}-\frac{8}{15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8\ \times \ 5}{90}-\frac{8\ \times \ 6}{90}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{90}-\frac{48}{90} {/tex} = {tex}-\frac{8}{90} \neq \frac{8}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The above condition is false

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{7}{12}+\frac{11}{12}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}.

options.explanation: True

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{16}{25}>\frac{13}{25}{/tex}.

options.explanation: True

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: {tex}\frac{11}{16}{/tex} ________ {tex}\frac{14}{15}{/tex} using >, < or = :
Fill in the blank: {tex}\frac{8}{15}{/tex} ________ {tex}\frac{95}{14}{/tex} using >, < or = :
Fill in the blank: {tex}\frac{12}{75}{/tex} ________ {tex}\frac{32}{200}{/tex} using >, < or =:
Write the fraction represented by the shaded portion of the adjoining figure:
questions.solution_html:
In the given figure, total parts in which figure has been divided in 8 and out of which 7 parts are shaded
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction is {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Write the fraction represented by the unshaded portion of the adjoining figure:
questions.solution_html:
In the given figure, the total parts in which the figure has been divided into 15 and out of which 4 parts are unshaded.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction is {tex}\frac{4}{15}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Ali divided one fruit cake equally among six persons. What part of the cake did he give to each person?
questions.solution_html:
Since Ali has to divide one fruit cake equally among 6 persons
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Each person will get {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} part.

questions.question_html: Express {tex}6 \frac{2}{3}{/tex} as an improper fraction.
questions.solution_html: We have, {tex}6 \frac{2}{3}=\frac{(6 \ \times \ 3)\ + \ 2}{3} = \frac{18\ + \ 2}{3}=\frac{20}{3}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Express 0.041 as a fraction.
questions.solution_html: We have, {tex}0.041=\frac{41}{1000} {/tex}.

questions.question_html: Express 6.03 as a mixed fraction.
questions.solution_html:
We have, {tex}6.03=\frac{603}{100}{/tex}
{tex}=6 \frac{3}{100}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Convert 2009 paise to rupees and express the result as a mixed fraction.
questions.solution_html:
We have,
2009 paise = {tex}\text { ₹ } \frac{2009}{100}{/tex} = ₹20.09

{tex}\mathrm{₹ } 20 \frac{9}{100}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Convert 1537cm to m and express the result as an improper fraction.
questions.solution_html: We have, 1537 cm = {tex}\frac{1537}{100}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Convert 2435m to km and express the result as a mixed fraction.
questions.solution_html:
We have, 2435 m = {tex}\frac{2435}{1000} {/tex}km
{tex}[\because{/tex} 1 km = 1000 m]
= 2.435 km
Firstly, convert the fraction {tex} \frac{2435}{1000}{/tex} into the simplest form, for this
dividing the numerator and denominator by 5, we get {tex}\frac{2435 \div 5}{1000 \div 5} {/tex} km = {tex}\frac{487}{200}{/tex} km

i.e., {tex}2 \frac{87}{200}{/tex}km

questions.question_html: Arrange the fractions {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} in ascending order.
questions.solution_html:
We have given, {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
Firstly find the L.C.M. of 3, 4, 2 and 6

The L.C.M. of 3, 4, 2 and 6 is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 12
Now, {tex}\frac{2}{3}=\frac{2 \times 4}{3 \times 4}=\frac{8}{12}, \frac{3}{4}=\frac{3 \times 3}{4 \times 3}=\frac{9}{12}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1 \times 6}{2 \times 6}=\frac{6}{12}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{6}=\frac{5 \times 2}{6 \times 2}=\frac{10}{12}{/tex}
On comparing the above, we get
{tex}\frac{6}{12}<\frac{8}{12}<\frac{9}{12}<\frac{10}{12}{/tex}
i.e., {tex}\frac{1}{2}<\frac{2}{3}<\frac{3}{4}<\frac{5}{6}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Arrange the fractions {tex}\frac{6}{7}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} in descending order.
questions.solution_html:
Given fractions are: {tex}\frac{6}{7}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
The L.C.M. of 7, 8, 5 and 4 is
2 {tex}\times {/tex} 2 {tex}\times {/tex} 2 {tex}\times {/tex} 5 {tex}\times {/tex} 7 = 280
Now, {tex}\frac{6}{7}=\frac{6 \ \times \ 40}{7 \ \times \ 40}=\frac{240}{280}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{7}{8}=\frac{7 \ \times \ 35}{8 \ \times \ 35} = \frac{245}{280}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{4}{5}=\frac{4 \ \times \ 56}{5\ \times \ 56}=\frac{224}{280}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3\ \times \ 70}{4 \ \times \ 70}=\frac{210}{280}{/tex}
On comparing the above, we get;
{tex}\frac{245}{280}>\frac{240}{280}>\frac{224}{280}>\frac{210}{280}{/tex}
i.e., {tex}\frac{7}{8}>\frac{6}{7}>\frac{4}{5}>\frac{3}{4}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Write {tex}3 \over4{/tex} as a fraction with denominator 44.
questions.solution_html:
Let {tex}\frac{3}{4}\ =\ \frac{?}{44}{/tex}
Then, we have to find the missing numeral
To get 44 in the denominator, we multiply 4 by 11
So, we multiply the numerator and denominator by 11
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3\ \times \ 11}{4\ \times \ 11}=\frac{33}{44}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{33}{44}{/tex} are equivalent fractions.

questions.question_html: Write {tex}5 \over6{/tex} as a fraction with numerator 60.
questions.solution_html:
Let {tex}\frac{5}{6}=\frac{60}{?}{/tex}
Then, we have to find the missing numeral. To get 60 in the numerator, we multiply 5 by 12.
So, we multiply the numerator and denominator by 12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{5\ \times \ 12}{6 \ \times \ 12}=\frac{60}{72}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{60}{72}{/tex} are equivalent fractions.

questions.question_html: Write {tex}\frac{129}{8}{/tex} as a mixed fraction.
questions.solution_html: We have, {tex}\frac{129}{8}=16 \frac{1}{8}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Add the fractions {tex}3 \over8{/tex} and {tex}2 \over3 {/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
L.C.M. of 8 and 3 is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 24

Now, {tex}\frac{3}{8}+\frac{2}{3}=\frac{3\ \times \ 3}{8\ \times \ 3}+\frac{2 \ \times \ 8}{3 \ \times \ 8}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9}{24}+\frac{16}{24}=\frac{9\ +\ 16}{24}=\frac{25}{24}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Add the fractions {tex}3 \over8{/tex} and {tex}6 \frac{3}{4}{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
L.C.M of 8 and 4 is

2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8
Now, {tex}\frac{3}{8}+6 \frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{8}+\frac{27}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{8}+\frac{27 \times 2}{4 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{8}+\frac{54}{8}=\frac{3+54}{8}=\frac{57}{8}=7 \frac{1}{8}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Subtract {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} from {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
The L.C.M of 6 and 2 = 6

Now, {tex}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1\ \times \ 3}{2\ \times\ 3}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{3}{6}-\frac{1}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3-1}{6}=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{2\ \div \ 2}{6\ \div\ 2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} H.C.F. of 2 and 6 is 2]
= {tex}\frac{1}{3} {/tex}.

questions.question_html: Subtract {tex}8 \frac{1}{3}{/tex} from {tex}100 \over9{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
The L.C.M of 3 and 9 = 9
i.e;

Now, {tex}\frac{100}{9}-8 \frac{1}{3}=\frac{100}{9}-\frac{25}{3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{100}{9}-\frac{25 \ \times \ 3}{3 \ \times \ 3}=\frac{100}{9}-\frac{75}{9}=\frac{100\ -\ 75}{9}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \frac{7}{9}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Subtract {tex}1 \frac{1}{4}{/tex} from {tex}6 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
The L.C.M of 4 and 2 = 4
Now, {tex}6 \frac{1}{2}-1 \frac{1}{4}=\frac{13}{2}-\frac{5}{4}{/tex}

{tex}=\frac{13\ \times \ 2}{2 \ \times \ 2}-\frac{5}{4}=\frac{26}{4}-\frac{5}{4}=\frac{26\ - \ 5}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{21}{4}=5 \frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Add{tex}1 \frac{1}{4}{/tex} to{tex}6 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
The L.C.M. of 4 and 2 = 4

Now, {tex}1\frac{1}{4}+6\frac{1}{2}=\frac {5}{4} + \frac {13}{2} {/tex}
{tex}= \frac{5}{4}+\frac{13 \times 2}{2 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac {5}{4} + \frac {26}{4} =\frac{5+26}{4} = \frac {31}4 = 7\frac34{/tex}

questions.question_html: Katrina rode her bicycle {tex}6 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} km in the morning and {tex}8 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} km in the evening. Find the distance travelled by her altogether on that day.
questions.solution_html:
The distance travelled by Katrina in the morning {tex}=6 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{km}=\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{km}{/tex}
The distance travelled by Katrina in the evening {tex}=8 \frac{3}{4} \mathrm{km}=\frac{35}{4} \mathrm{km}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total distance travelled by her
{tex}=\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{km}+\frac{35}{4} \mathrm{km}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{13 \times 2}{2 \times 2} \mathrm{km}+\frac{35}{4} \mathrm{km}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M. of 2 and 4 = 4]
{tex}=\frac{26}{4} \mathrm{km}+\frac{35}{4} \mathrm{km}=\frac{(26\ +\ 35) \mathrm{km}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{61}{4} \mathrm{km}=15 \frac{1}{4} \mathrm{km}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: A rectangle is divided into a certain number of equal parts. If 16 of the parts so formed represent the fraction {tex}1 \over4{/tex}, find the number of parts in which the rectangle has been divided.
questions.solution_html:
Let the number of parts in which the rectangle has been divided be x. According to question, {tex}\frac{1}{4}=\frac{16}{x}{/tex}
By cross-multiplication, x = 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 64
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required number of parts is 64.

questions.question_html: Grip size of a tennis racquet is {tex}11 \frac{9}{80}{/tex} cm. Express the size as an improper fraction.
questions.solution_html: We have given, the grip size of a tennis racquet = {tex}11 \frac{9}{80} \mathrm{cm}=\frac{889}{80} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}, which is the required improper fraction.

questions.question_html: On an average {tex}1 \over10{/tex} of the food eaten is turned into organism’s own body and is available for the next level of consumer in a food chain. What fraction of the food eaten is not available for the next level?
questions.solution_html:
We have given, {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} of the food eaten is turned into an organism's own body.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction of the food eaten not available for the next level is {tex}1-\frac{1}{10}=\frac{10-1}{10}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} LCM of 1 and 10 is 10]
= {tex}\frac{9}{10}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Mr. Rajan got a job at the age of 24 years and he got retired from the job at the age of 60 years. What fraction of his age till retirement was he in the job?
questions.solution_html:
Mr. Rajan got a job at the age of 24 years
He got retired at the age of 60 years.
He worked for (60 - 24) years = 36 years
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction = {tex}\frac{36}{60}=\frac{36 \ \div \ 12}{600012}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} H.C.F. of 36 and 60 is 12]

questions.question_html: The food we eat remains in the stomach for a maximum of 4 hours. For what fraction of a day, does it remain there?
questions.solution_html:
The food we eat remains in the stomach for a maximum of 4 hours
Total number of hours in a day = 24 hours
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction ={tex}\frac{4}{24}=\frac{4\ \div \ 4}{24\ \div\ 4}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
[{tex}\because {/tex} H.C.F of 4 and 24 is 4]

questions.question_html:
It was estimated that because of people switching to Metro trains, about 33000 tonnes of CNG, 3300 tonnes of diesel and 21000 tonnes of petrol were saved by the end of the year 2007. Find the fraction of :

  1. the quantity of diesel saved to the quantity of petrol saved.
  2. the quantity of diesel saved to the quantity of CNG saved.
questions.solution_html:
By the end of year 2007 The quantity of CNG saved 33000 tonnes,
The quantity of diesel saved 3300 tonnes and The quantity of petrol saved 21000 tonnes

  1. {tex}\frac{\text { The quantity of diesel saved }}{\text { The quantity of petrol saved }}=\frac{3300}{21000}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{3300 \div 300}{21000 \div 300}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} H.C.F. of 3300 and 21000 is 300]
    {tex}=\frac{11}{70}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{\text { The quantity of diesel saved }}{\text { The quantity of CNG saved }}=\frac{3300}{33000}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{3300\div3300}{33000\div3300}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} H.C.F. of 3300 and 33000 is 3300]
    {tex}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}

questions.question_html: A cup is {tex}1 \over3{/tex} full of milk. What part of the cup is still to be filled with milk to make it full?
questions.solution_html:
A cup is {tex} \frac{1}{3}{/tex} full of milk.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The remaining part of the cup which is still to be filled by milk = {tex}1-\frac{1}{3} =\frac{3\ -\ 1}{3}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M of 1 and 3 is 3] = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Mary bought {tex}3 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} m of lace. She used {tex}1 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} m of lace for her new dress. How much lace is left with her?
questions.solution_html:
Mary bought the lace = {tex}3 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
Lace used by Mary ={tex}1 \frac{3}{4} \mathrm{m}=\frac{7}{4} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} She is left with {tex}\frac{7}{2} m-\frac{7}{4} m{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7 \times 2}{2 \times 2} \mathrm{m}-\frac{7}{4} \mathrm{m}=\frac{14 \mathrm{m}-7 \mathrm{m}}{4}{/tex}
[{tex}\because {/tex} L . C . M . of 2 and 4 is 4] ={tex}\frac{7}{4} \mathrm{m}=1 \frac{3}{4} \mathrm{m}{/tex} of lace.

questions.question_html: When Sunita weighed herself on Monday, she found that she had gained {tex}1 \frac{1}{4}{/tex} kg. Earlier her weight was {tex}46 \frac{3}{8}{/tex} kg. What was her weight on Monday?
questions.solution_html:
Sunita had gained = 1 {tex}\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{kg}=\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}
Earlier her weight was {tex}46 \frac{3}{8} k g=\frac{377}{8} k g{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Her total weight on Monday
{tex}=\frac{371}{8} \mathrm{kg}+\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{371}{8} \mathrm{kg}+\frac{5 \times 2}{4 \times 2} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M . of 8 and 4 is 8]
{tex}=\frac{371}{8} \mathrm{kg}+\frac{10}{8} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(371+10)}{8} \mathrm{kg}=\frac{381}{8} \mathrm{kg}=47 \frac{5}{8} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Sunil purchased {tex}12 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} litres of juice on Monday and {tex}14 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} litres of juice on Tuesday. How many litres of juice did he purchase together in two days?
questions.solution_html:
Sunil purchased juice on Monday
= {tex}12 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} litres = {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex}litres
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total quantity of juice Sunil purchased in two days ={tex}\left(\frac{25}{2}+\frac{59}{4}\right){/tex} litres
{tex}=\left(\frac{25 \ \times \ 2}{2 \ \times\ 2}+\frac{59}{4}\right) \text { litres }=\left(\frac{50}{4}+\frac{59}{4}\right) \text { litres }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M. of 2 and 4 is 4]
= {tex}\left(\frac{50\ + \ 59}{4}\right){/tex} litres = {tex}\frac{109}{4}{/tex} litres = {tex}27 \frac{1}{4}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Nazima gave {tex}2 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} litres out of the {tex}5 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} litres of juice she purchased to her friends. How many litres of juice is left with her?
questions.solution_html:
Total quantity of juice {tex}=5 \frac{1}{2} {/tex} litres = {tex}\frac{11}{2} {/tex}litres
Nazima gave to her friends ={tex}2 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} litres = {tex}\frac{11}{4}{/tex} litres
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required quantity of juice she is left
with {tex}=\left(\frac{11}{2}-\frac{11}{4}\right){/tex} litres = {tex}\left(\frac{11\ \times \ 2}{2\ \times\ 2}-\frac{11}{4}\right){/tex} litres
{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M. of 2 and 4 is
= {tex}\left(\frac{22\ -\ 11}{4}\right){/tex}litres = {tex}2 \frac{3}{4} {/tex}litres.

questions.question_html: Roma gave a wooden board of length {tex}150 \frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm to a carpenter for making a shelf. The Carpenter sawed off a piece of {tex}40 \frac{1}{5}{/tex} cm from it. What is the length of the remaining piece?
questions.solution_html:
Total length of a wooden board
{tex}=150 \frac{1}{4} \mathrm{cm}=\frac{601}{4} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The length of the remaining piece
{tex}=\left(\frac{601}{4}-\frac{201}{5}\right) \mathrm{cm}=\left(\frac{601\ \times \ 5}{4\ \times \ 5}-\frac{201 \ \times \ 4}{5 \ \times \ 4}\right) \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M. of 4 and 5 is 20]
{tex}=\left(\frac{3005}{20}-\frac{804}{20}\right) \mathrm{cm}=\frac{2201}{20} \mathrm{cm} {/tex} or {tex}110 \frac{1}{20} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Nasir travelled {tex}3 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} km in a bus and then walked {tex}1 \frac{1}{8}{/tex} km to reach a town. How much did he travel to reach the town?
questions.solution_html:
Nasir travelled by a bus = {tex}3 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} km = {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} km
Nasir walked = {tex}1 \frac{1}{8}{/tex} km = {tex}\frac{9}{8}{/tex} km
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total distance travelled by him
{tex}=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{km}+\frac{9}{8} \mathrm{km} =\frac{7 \ \times \ 4}{2 \ \times \ 4} \mathrm{km}+\frac{9}{8} \mathrm{km} {/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} L.C.M. of 2 and 8 is 8]
={tex}\left(\frac{28}{8}+\frac{9}{8}\right){/tex} km
={tex}\left(\frac{28\ + \ 9}{8}\right) \mathrm{km}=\frac{37}{8} \mathrm{km} {/tex} or {tex}4 \frac{5}{8} \mathrm{km}{/tex}

questions.question_html: The fish caught by Neetu was of weight {tex}3 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} kg and the fish caught by Narendra was of weight {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} kg. How much more did Neetu’s fish weigh than that of Narendra?
questions.solution_html:
The weight of fish caught by Neetu
= {tex}3 \frac{3}{4} {/tex} kg = {tex}\frac{15}{4} {/tex}kg
The weight of fish caught by Narendra = 2{tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex} kg = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} kg
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Neetu's fish weigh more than that of Narendra by
{tex}\frac{15}{4}{/tex}kg - {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} kg ={tex}\frac{15}{4}{/tex} kg - {tex}\frac{5\ \times \ 2}{2\ \times\ 2} {/tex}kg = {tex}\frac{15}{4} {/tex} kg - {tex}\frac{10}{4} {/tex}kg [{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M. of 4 and 2 is 4]
= {tex}\frac{(15\ -\ 10)}{4}{/tex} kg = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex}kg or {tex}1 \frac{1}{4}{/tex} kg.

questions.question_html: Neelam’s father needs {tex}1 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} m of cloth for the skirt of Neelam’s new dress and {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} m for the scarf. How much cloth must he buy in all?
questions.solution_html:
Neelam's father purchased the length of cloth for the skirt = {tex}1 \frac{3}{4}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{7}{4}{/tex} m and for the scarf = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total length he buys in all ={tex}\frac{7}{4} \mathrm{m}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{7}{4} m+\frac{1\ \times\ 2}{2\ \times\ 2} m=\frac{7}{4} m+\frac{2}{4} m{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} L.C.M . of 4 and 2 is 4] ={tex}\frac{(7\ +\ 2)}{4}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{9}{4}{/tex} m or 2 {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} m

questions.question_html: What is wrong in the given addition?
questions.solution_html:
In the above addition numerators and denominators get added directly without rationalising the mixed fractions.
Hence the correct addition will be:
{tex}8\frac 12 + 4 \frac 14{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {17}2 + \frac {17} 4{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {34+17} 4{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {51}4{/tex}
= {tex}12\frac {3}4{/tex}

questions.question_html: What is wrong with the addition?
questions.solution_html:
Numerators and denominators get added directly without rationalising the mixed fractions so the result obtained is incorrect.
Correct addition will be:
{tex}6\frac 12 + 2 \frac 14{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {13}2 + \frac {9} 4{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {26+9} 4{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {35}4{/tex}
= {tex}8\frac {3}4{/tex}

questions.question_html:
Match the fractions of Column I with the shaded or marked portion of figures of Column II:

Column I Column II
(a) {tex}\frac{6}{4}{/tex} (i) 
(b) {tex}\frac{6}{ 10}{/tex} (ii)
(c) {tex}\frac{6}{6}{/tex} (iii) 
(d) {tex}\frac{6}{16}{/tex} (iv) 
  (v)
Find the fraction that represents the number of natural numbers to total numbers in the collection 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. What fraction will it be for whole numbers?
questions.solution_html:
Out of 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 are the natural numbers.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The fraction that represents the number of natural numbers to the total numbers = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
and the whole numbers are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The fraction that represents the number of whole numbers to the total numbers = {tex}\frac{6}{6}{/tex}

questions.question_html: Write the fraction representing the total number of natural numbers in the collection of numbers –3, –2, –1, 0, 1, 2, 3. What fraction will it be for whole numbers? What fraction will it be for integers?
questions.solution_html:
Out of -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, 2 and 3 are the natural numbers, 0, 1, 2 and 3 are the whole
numbers and -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3 are integers.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The fraction representing the natural numbers to the total numbers = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}
The fraction representing the whole numbers to the total numbers ={tex}\frac{4}{7}{/tex}
And the required fraction representing the integers to the total numbers = {tex}\frac{7}{7}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Write a pair of fractions whose sum is {tex}7 \over11{/tex} and difference is {tex}2 \over11{/tex}.
questions.solution_html:
Let one fraction be x
Another fraction be {tex}\frac{7}{11}-x{/tex}
Now, according to question,
{tex}x-\left(\frac{7}{11}-x\right)=\frac{2}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x-\frac{7}{11}+x=\frac{2}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{11 x}{11}-\frac{7}{11}+\frac{11 x}{11}=\frac{2}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{11 x\ -\ 7\ + \ 11 x}{11}=\frac{2}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 22 x-7=\frac{2}{11} \times 11{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 22x - 7 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 22x = 2 + 7 = 9
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{9}{22}{/tex}
Thus, one fraction is {tex}\frac{9}{22}{/tex} and another fraction is
{tex}\frac{7}{11}-\frac{9}{22}=\frac{7 \ \times \ 2}{11\ \times\ 2}-\frac{9}{22}=\frac{14\ -\ 9}{22}=\frac{5}{22}{/tex}

questions.question_html: What fraction of a straight angle is a right angle?
questions.solution_html:
Since we know that the measurement of a straight angle is 180o and the right angle is 90o.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required fraction is {tex} \frac{90^{\circ}}{180^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.

questions.question_html:
Put the right card in the right bag.

Cards  Bags
(i) {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} Bag I
(ii) {tex}\frac{4}{4}{/tex}
(iii) {tex}\frac{9}{8}{/tex}
(iv) {tex}\frac{8}{9}{/tex} Bag II
(v) {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
(vi) {tex}\frac{6}{11} {/tex}
(vii) {tex}\frac{18}{18}{/tex} Bag III
(viii) {tex}\frac{19}{25}{/tex}
(ix) {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
(x) {tex}\frac{13}{17}{/tex}
questions.solution_html:
We know that if numerator is less than the denominator, then the fraction is less than 1
If the numerator is equal to the denominator, then the fraction is equal to 1 and if the numerator is greater than the denominator, then the fraction is greater than 1
Cards in Bag I are
(i) {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}
(iv) {tex}\frac{8}{9}{/tex}
(v) {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
(vi) {tex}\frac{6}{11}{/tex}
(viii) {tex}\frac{19}{25}{/tex}
(ix) {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and
(x) {tex}\frac{13}{17}{/tex}
Cards in Bag II are (ii) {tex}\frac{4}{4}{/tex}, (vii) {tex}\frac{18}{18}{/tex} And cards in Bag III are (iii) {tex}\frac{9}{8}{/tex}.
